title: 'Appalling' Darwin Port plans criticised by treasurer who leased it
author: Matt Garrick and David O'Shea
fullText: The former Northern Territory treasurer who leased Darwin Port to a Chinese-owned company says he would "absolutely" make the same decision again if he had his time over.Former NT government minister Dave Tollner was part of the Country Liberal Party (CLP) cabinet that leased the facility to Landbridge for $506 million in 2015.The port's 99-year lease has become the centre of a geopolitical debate in the past decade, with the federal government promising to get the lease "back into Australian hands".Mr Tollner branded Prime Minister Anthony Albanese's pre-election promise to take the lease back as an "appalling waste of money"."I can just see a monumental stuff-up occurring," Mr Tollner said."You know, no matter what people think of the Chinese, we've done a commercial deal with Landbridge … Landbridge have done a cracker job."It's not a spy nest."Two national security reviews and a parliamentary inquiry have been undertaken on the port lease but none have ever produced any public findings of a security threat.Dave Tollner has criticised the Australian government's plan to cut short the 99-year deal. (ABC News: Marcus Kennedy)Mr Tollner also criticised a similar promise to take back the lease, made by former opposition leader Peter Dutton prior to the last federal election, as a "silly thing" that was overtly political."[Getting the lease back] will literally cost billions … that could be put into something much, much better," Mr Tollner said."I mean, it's a tiny little port."It serves the needs of Darwin … to [suggest it could be used] for large-scale military operation seems absolutely ludicrous."Exactly how much the pending Port of Darwin lease deal could cost taxpayers is unclear, with no potential buyback price for the lease made public by Landbridge.In response to Mr Tollner's comments, the prime minister's office pointed to Mr Albanese's repeated criticisms of the former NT government's decision to lease the port.Darwin Port is being leased by Landbridge, but Mr Tollner suggested a separate site — north of the city — be considered for a new facility. (ABC News: Tristan Hooft)The Commonwealth's decision to take back the lease has also been welcomed by politicians in the US.During a recent visit to Darwin, Democratic Senator Tim Kaine said US officials were "heartened by the news" the government had committed to bringing the port back under Australian control.'World was a different place' in 2015Mr Tollner was critical of what he described as the Commonwealth's politicisation of an aging facility, which he said was "literally sinking into the harbour" when Landbridge leased it.When asked if he would lease it to the Chinese-owned firm again, knowing what he knows now, Mr Tollner said he would.Geopolitical relations have shifted since the port's 99-year lease was signed. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)"Absolutely, we didn't have a choice," he said."Look, the whole world was a different place then."The Chinese are [seen as] quite a bit more of a threat than we realised they were back then — you've got to remember, we had a good trading relationship with the Chinese."The NT government's decision to lease the port in 2015 has been roundly criticised by successive prime ministers, including Mr Albanese, who has repeatedly said he never would have "flogged it off in the first place".But Mr Tollner argued the federal government of the day ticked off on the deal."We got all of the strategic information out of Defence about security issues and that sort of stuff … they signed off on it," he said."It was only at the lead-up of the last election that that changed."Darwin Port has been the subject of controversy for years, but no security threats have been identified since Landbridge took over the lease. (ABC News: Che Chorley)Glyde Point suggested for new facilityThe former CLP treasurer is calling on the federal government to shift its focus away from the current Darwin Port to an area outside the city called Glyde Point. Mr Tollner said the coastal spot — located north of Darwin, below the Tiwi Islands — could facilitate a future port to be used by the Australian Defence Force and its allies, including the US.Bill Yan says his government is focused on developing the new 246-hectare Northern Marine Complex on Darwin Harbour. (ABC News: Michael Franchi)"There's hundreds of square kilometres of land out there, adjacent land," he said."Everything's there, the road corridors, rail corridors, pipeline corridors, it's all been planned."I think, as a military installation … as a shared facility, it would be available for the US, Australia and all of our allies to sort of piggyback on."Current NT Treasurer and Infrastructure Minister Bill Yan did not comment directly on whether his government supported Mr Tollner's ideas about Glyde Point, but said it was focused on delivering a new marine precinct at East Arm in Darwin."The CLP will deliver the ship lift facility and marine industry park, establishing Darwin as a competitive, full-service maritime hub alongside Darwin Port," he said.The facility is based in Darwin's East Arm. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)Mr Yan also called for clarity around the Albanese government's plans to take back the port lease."The ongoing uncertainty is having an impact on the local workforce and industry, which is why a quick resolution is required," he said.No timeline has been given on when the port lease will be taken back, however the Commonwealth has repeatedly said it is engaged in commercial negotiations over the prospect.
summary: Former NT treasurer Dave Tollner describes the federal government's plans to take back the 99-year lease of Darwin Port as "an appalling waste of money".
